title: Body in Perimenopause: What Shifts
subtitle: A reading on the physical changes of perimenopause and what they mean for how you work with your body
excerpt: A reading on the perimenopause transition and what it means for the relationship between a woman and her body. Covers what changes, why the changes happen, and how to work with the body rather than against it during this phase.
sourceNotes: Written for homemade.education. Draws on public-domain perimenopause research and the work of Dr Louise Newson and others in expanding perimenopause awareness.

practice.whenToUse: Use when you are aware that your body is changing in ways that don't match the life you were used to, and you want a practical account of what is happening.

content.text: A reading on what happens to the body in perimenopause and what it means for day-to-day living. Perimenopause can begin in the late thirties and typically runs for several years before menopause. The changes are wide-ranging and are still under-discussed in mainstream health contexts, which means many women arrive at the transition without a useful account of what is happening.

Perimenopause is the transition period before menopause, during which oestrogen, progesterone, and testosterone levels shift and fluctuate. It is not a single event but a process, and its length and character vary considerably between women. Some women notice changes for a year or two. Others are in the transition for a decade.

Menopause itself is defined as the point after twelve consecutive months without a period. Everything before that is perimenopause, even if the changes are significant. The cultural use of 'menopause' to describe the whole transition is imprecise, and matters because the perimenopausal phase is where most of the symptoms occur.

The better-known changes include irregular periods, hot flushes, night sweats, and disrupted sleep. Less widely discussed changes include: changes to mood and anxiety levels (oestrogen has a moderating effect on the stress response, so its reduction can increase anxiety and irritability); changes to memory and concentration (sometimes called 'brain fog'); changes to skin, hair, and body composition; joint pain; and changes to libido and sexual comfort.

The range of symptoms is broad enough that many women do not recognise them as perimenopausal until they are well into the transition. Anxiety and mood changes, in particular, are frequently treated as mental health problems without the hormonal context being considered.
Why the relationship with the body changes

Women who have had a reliable, predictable body, whose physical capacity and energy patterns they have planned around, often find the perimenopause transition disorienting precisely because the body they knew is not behaving in expected ways. The energy that was available at a certain time of day is no longer reliable. The sleep pattern has changed. The body composition has shifted. The rules that worked before don't work in the same way.

This is not a failure of the body. It is a transition. But it requires a renegotiation of the relationship with the body: what it needs now, what it can do now, what needs adjusting in how it is managed and supported.
Working with the body rather than against it

The practical adjustments that tend to help are specific rather than general. Sleep is often the most immediate: addressing sleep disruption directly (through sleep hygiene, addressing night sweats, considering HRT if appropriate) improves almost everything else. Exercise continues to matter, but the type may shift: high-intensity work that served well in the thirties may not suit the body in the same way in the forties, and strength work becomes more important for bone density and muscle mass.
Food and alcohol sensitivity often change. Things that were manageable before may now create more disruption. Tracking what affects how the body feels is more productive than applying generic advice.

The psychological piece is less often named: accepting that the body is in a transition rather than a decline. The perimenopause body is not broken. It is doing something that takes energy, and the demands on it need to account for that.

Written for homemade.education. The reading draws on public-domain perimenopause research and work by clinicians including Dr Louise Newson, whose advocacy has significantly expanded perimenopause awareness in the UK, and the British Menopause Society's clinical guidance on perimenopause symptoms.
